headline: Teen's AI App Turns Citizens Into Road Safety Activists
meta_description: A 15-year-old in New Delhi built an app that lets citizens report potholes and automatically sends complaints to authorities, bypassing bureaucratic delays.
summary_short: After his parents were injured in a motorcycle accident caused by a pothole, Parth created Project Sadak—an AI app that lets New Delhi residents report road damage insta…
body:
When Parth's parents had a motorcycle accident on a New Delhi pothole while returning from a family celebration in Agra, the teenager decided to turn tragedy into action. The 15-year-old, already fascinated by science and technology, began building Project Sadak—an AI-powered app that transforms how citizens report road damage.

The platform works simply: users photograph a pothole, the app uses GPS to pinpoint its location, and AI verifies it's actually a pothole rather than a false report. Human reviewers then grade severity as severe, medium, or benign, ensuring the worst damage gets priority attention rather than being buried in complaints.

The real innovation comes next. Instead of citizens hunting for email addresses and extension numbers, Project Sadak automatically drafts and submits complaints to local authorities on their behalf, complete with a digital signature.

"Initially, we were emailing the parties manually," Parth explained. "But now the platform automatically writes an email and submits the report."

So far, 360 potholes have been reported through the app. While only 11 have been repaired—mostly through Parth's own efforts using his father's construction contacts—one report even came from Bengaluru, where authorities fixed the damage despite the app having no pre-loaded contact information.

Parth envisions a WhatsApp chatbot version, making the app accessible without downloads, and full automation from photo to submitted complaint.
